Are there different ways of existing (physical objects, mathematical truths, social institutions, fictional characters)? These questions about existence and being have occupied philosophers from Parmenides and Plato to contemporary analytic metaphysics — and they have unexpected relevance to economics and the social sciences.

section_1.heading: The Ontological Question
section_1.content: Aristotle distinguished different 'categories' of being — substance, quality, quantity, relation — as different ways something can exist. Contemporary metaphysics has multiplied these questions. Do abstract objects like numbers and sets exist? Mathematical Platonists say yes — numbers are real entities, not merely useful symbols. Nominalists say no — only particular physical things exist; numbers are convenient fictions. The dispute matters for economics: are economic models (like 'perfect competition' or 'rational agents') describing real structures in the world, or are they useful idealisations without independent existence?

section_2.heading: Possible Worlds and Modal Reality
section_2.content: One of the most productive tools in modern metaphysics is the concept of possible worlds — ways the world could have been. When we say something is possible, we mean it is true in at least one possible world; when we say it is necessary, we mean it is true in all possible worlds. This framework, developed by Saul Kripke and David Lewis, has been extraordinarily fruitful for logic, language, and philosophy. For economics, counterfactual reasoning — 'what would have happened if the policy had not been implemented?' — is essential to causal analysis. Possible worlds offer a formal framework for making such counterfactuals rigorous.

section_3.heading: Social Construction and Realism
section_3.content: A central debate in the social sciences and metaphysics concerns social constructivism: are categories like race, gender, economic class, or national identity 'real' or 'merely socially constructed'? The metaphysical question is whether these are natural kinds (with mind-independent boundaries) or social kinds (whose existence depends on human practices and classifications). The answer matters: if 'race' has no biological reality but is a social construct, this shapes how we understand racial inequality and what remedies are appropriate. Social constructivism need not imply that socially constructed entities are unreal or unimportant — money is constructed, but it is not therefore trivial.

key_takeaways: Ontology asks what exists and how — whether abstract objects, social entities, and possibilities are real.
Mathematical Platonism vs. nominalism asks whether numbers are real or merely useful symbols.
Possible worlds provide a formal framework for necessity, possibility, and counterfactual reasoning.
Social constructivism argues that many categories are constituted by human practices, not nature.
A 'constructed' entity (like money or race) can be genuinely real and causally significant.

real_world_examples.title: The Reality of Economic Models
real_world_examples.description: Is 'perfect competition' a real phenomenon or a useful fiction? Economists disagree. Instrumentalists hold that models need only generate accurate predictions, regardless of whether their assumptions are realistic. Realists hold that good models should accurately represent underlying economic structures. This is a metaphysical dispute about what economic theory is doing.

real_world_examples.title: Race as a Social Kind
real_world_examples.description: Geneticists have shown that there is more genetic variation within racial groups than between them — suggesting race has no deep biological basis. Yet race is real in its social consequences: it shapes access to jobs, housing, education, and legal treatment. This illustrates the philosophical point that social construction does not equal social unreality.

meta-free-will.subject: Philosophy

expanded_explanation.introduction: The problem of free will is one of philosophy's oldest and most practically consequential puzzles. Do human beings have genuine freedom of choice, or are our decisions the inevitable product of prior causes — genes, upbringing, brain states, and the laws of physics? The answer matters for ethics (can we be genuinely responsible for our actions?), law (does punishment make sense if we couldn't have chosen otherwise?), and economics (are 'preferences' genuine expressions of free agency or the output of causal processes we don't control?).

section_1.heading: The Determinist Challenge
section_1.content: Hard determinism holds that every event — including every human decision — is the inevitable consequence of prior causes operating according to natural laws. Given the state of the universe at any moment and the laws of physics, everything that follows (including our 'choices') is fixed in advance. If determinism is true, then in a deep sense we could not have done otherwise than we did. This seems to undermine moral responsibility: how can we praise or blame people for actions they had no real choice about? Compatibilists respond that the kind of freedom that matters morally is not the freedom from causal determination but the freedom to act on one's own desires and reasons without external compulsion.

section_2.heading: Compatibilism: Freedom Within Causation
section_2.content: Compatibilism is the dominant position in contemporary philosophy. It holds that free will and determinism are not in conflict — what matters for freedom is whether your actions flow from your own deliberation, values, and character, not whether those deliberations are causally determined. A person who acts under coercion (gunpoint) is not free; a person who deliberates and acts on their own values is free, even if their deliberation was causally determined by prior brain states. This view has direct implications for economics: rational agency — the core assumption of economic theory — is compatible with a deterministic universe. What matters is the structure of the deliberative process, not its causal history.

section_3.heading: Free Will and Economic Behaviour
section_3.content: The free will debate connects to behavioural economics in a striking way. If our 'choices' are the products of cognitive biases, social contexts, and evolutionary pressures, are they genuinely free? When a nudge pushes someone toward a 'better' decision by exploiting their status quo bias, has it enhanced or undermined their autonomy? This is a live ethical and political debate about libertarian paternalism (covered in Economics Topic 4). More broadly, if human preferences are the product of marketing, social norms, and cultural conditioning rather than authentic individual reflection, does satisfying those preferences really maximise welfare? This question connects the metaphysics of free will directly to normative economics.

key_takeaways: Hard determinism: every decision is causally determined; this seems to undermine moral responsibility.
Compatibilism: free will and determinism are compatible; what matters is acting from your own reasons.
Libertarianism (not the political kind): some decisions are genuinely uncaused, preserving free will.
Free will connects to legal responsibility, punishment, and the meaning of moral praise and blame.
Behavioural economics raises a new version of the free will question: are nudged decisions freely made?

real_world_examples.title: Criminal Sentencing and Responsibility
real_world_examples.description: Neuroscience has shown that brain damage can dramatically alter behaviour, including criminal behaviour. Courts increasingly consider neuroscientific evidence in sentencing. This raises the free will question directly: if a person's violence is partly the product of a brain tumour, does this reduce their moral and legal responsibility? Compatibilists say it depends on whether the action flows from deliberation; hard determinists would push for therapy over punishment across the board.

real_world_examples.title: Addiction and Autonomy
real_world_examples.description: Is a person who is addicted to heroin making free choices? Standard economics would say yes — they are expressing preferences. Critics argue that addiction undermines the deliberative processes that make choice genuinely free. This connects to debates about paternalistic policy: should the state restrict access to addictive substances to protect future selves from present impulsive choices? The answer hinges on the metaphysics of freedom and the ethics of self-regarding action.
